---
title: "practicum_1.1"
output: html_document
date: "2026-03-04"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

# Practicum 1.1

## Conflicts

```{r}
library(conflicted)

conflict_prefer("filter", "dplyr")
conflict_prefer("lag", "dplyr")
conflict_prefer("intersect", "dplyr")
```

## Libraries

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(tibble)
library(magrittr)
library(conflicted)
```

## Loading Data

```{r}
# This automatically creates a tibble, which is tidyverse’s modern data frame.
players <- read_csv("data/players_22.csv")
#spec(players)
```

## Task Solutions

### Task 2

#### Number of rows and columns

```{r}
dim(players)
```

\

nrow(players) ncol(players)

#### Check if Data is labeled or unlabeled

Labeled data → has a target column for prediction/classification.

It contains columns like overall, value_eur, rating, or position → it’s labeled for predictive tasks.

```{r}
colnames(players)
```

#### Check if data is Homogenous or Heterogenous

Data contains various data types for instance *short_name* is of type chr wherease overall is of type num.

```{r}
spec(players)
```

#### Can Tibble Be Replaced With a Matrix or Array?

Both Matrix and Array require homogenous data. In previous section we found out the player data contains various data thus cannot be replaced with a matrix or array which also requires data to be multidimensional.

### Task 3

```{r}

# sapply -> iterates over players' columns and applies class function
# class determines the type of an object

table(sapply(players, class))

```

### Task 4 - PLayers with complete records

We used complete.cases() along with sum() to determine the count of players with complete records (rows where each column has a value). We **got 0 complete rows** which is very likely for fifa dataset because there are some missing values for almost every player.

This can be explained by the fact that dataset contains many attributes that are only relevant for certain players, such as:

-   **goalkeeper attributes** (goalkeeping_diving, goalkeeping_kicking, etc.)
-   **loans** (club_loaned_from)
-   **tags** (player_tags)
-   **ids** (nation_team_id)

```{r}
# complete.cases returns logical vector where each value indicates
# whether a player has complete record

# sum only includes players with value TRUE
incomplete_records_sum <- sum(complete.cases(players))

percent <- mean(complete.cases(players)) * 100

incomplete_records_sum
percent
```

```{r}

#colSums(is.na(players))

#res <- is.na(players)

# 
# colSums is equivivalent to sapply(players, function(x) sum(is.na(x)))
head(sort(colSums(is.na(players)), decreasing = TRUE), 10)

# we can also use
# colSums(is.na(players))

```

### Task 5 - Duplicates

#### Duplicate records

A **duplicate record** means an entire row is identical to another row.

```{r}
# duplicated() checks whether elements of a vector are identical to earlier elements.
sum(duplicated(players))
```

#### Duplicate columns

R has no direct built-in function for this. We can, however, simply transpose the dataframe and use duplicated() again.

```{r}

#sum(duplicated(as.list(players)))
sum(duplicated(t(players)))

# other solution
# convert players into list, which is a collections of columns
#sum(duplicated(as.list(players)))
```

### Task 6

#### Column Types

```{r}
numeric_cols <- sapply(players, is.numeric)
char_cols <- sapply(players, is.character)
factor_cols <- sapply(players, is.factor)
```

#### Range for numeric columns

```{r}

numeric_ranges <- sapply(players[numeric_cols], range, na.rm = TRUE)
numeric_ranges
```

#### Categorical columns

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# drop cols with high cardinality
# we keep players unless they are character and they contain > 100 disctinct values
players_clean <- players %>%
  select(where(~ is.character(.) &&  n_distinct(.) < 100 ))

#char_values <- sapply(players_clean, unique)
# we want to print the char columns ordered by cardinality
# across(everything(), n_distinct) applies the function n_distinct() to every column of players_clean.
# n_distinct() counts how many unique values each column has.
# summarise() reduces the dataset to one row (unique value counts for each col)

players_clean %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), n_distinct)) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything()) %>%
  arrange(value)

char_counts <- sort(sapply(players_clean, n_distinct))
char_values <- lapply(players_clean[names(char_counts)], unique)
char_values

#char_values

```

### Task 7

We can use *View* function to preview the data and check the columns.

```{r}
View(players)
```

The first half of the dataset consits of the following columns:

1)  **personal info** (name, age, dob, club_name, etc.)
2)  **physical traits** (weight, height, body_type, etc.)
3)  **club info** (club_name, club_joined, etc.)
4)  **nation info** (nation name, nation position, etc.)
5)  **overall ratings** (overall, potential)

The second half of the dataset consists of columns representing the more detailed **skill stats** (shooting, passing, skill_volley, etc.).

**Player performance metrics** are presented via overall ratings, skill stats and goalkeeper stats. These metrics are generally on a 0–100 scale.

**Non-informative columns** are those of high cardinality and dates like *player_id*, *dob*, *club_team_id*, etc.

#### Rearranging data

1.  **Identifiers & metadata** (sofifa_id, long_name, short_name, age)
2.  **Categorical descriptive info** (nationality, club_name, club_position, etc.)
3.  **Numeric performance metrics** (overall, potential, pace, dribbling, etc.)
4.  **High-cardinality or non-informative columns** (we can also remove them completely)

### Task 8

```{r}

#conflict_prefer("filter", "dplyr")

top_player <- players %>%
  filter(overall == max(overall, na.rm = TRUE))

top_player %>%
  select(long_name, club_name, club_position, overall, potential, value_eur, wage_eur, preferred_foot) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.character)) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = "attribute",
    values_to = "value"
  )
```

### Task 9

```{r}
# first select top 100 players by overall score

top100 <- players %>%
  arrange(desc(overall)) %>%
  head(100)

# count left and right-footed players

summary_left_foot <- top100 %>%
  group_by(preferred_foot) %>%
  summarise(
    count = n(),
    proportion = round(n() / nrow(top100), 2)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(count))

summary_left_foot
```

### Task 10

From the overall average results we can see that left-footed players have slightly higher overall and thus are more talented than their right-footed counterparts althought left-footed players are underrepresented.

```{r}
overall_avg <- players %>%
  group_by(preferred_foot) %>%
  summarise(
    overall_avg = mean(overall, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(overall_avg))

overall_avg
```

### Task 11

```{r}

youngest_elite_player <- players %>%
  select(short_name, overall, dob) %>%
  filter(overall >= 80) %>%
  arrange(desc(dob)) %>%
  head(1)

youngest_elite_player
```

### Task 12

```{r}

#table(players$preferred_foot)

hidden_gems <- players %>%
  select(short_name, age, value_eur, potential, preferred_foot) %>%
  filter(preferred_foot == 'Left', age < 35) %>%
  arrange(value_eur, desc(potential)) %>%
  head(n = 5)

hidden_gems
```

### Task 13 - Left-footed players

#### The best dribbler

```{r}
best_dribbler <- players %>%
  select(short_name, dribbling, preferred_foot) %>%
  filter(preferred_foot == 'Left') %>%
  arrange(desc(dribbling)) %>%
  head(n = 1)

best_dribbler
```

#### Best shooters

```{r}
best_shooters <- players %>%
  filter(shooting >= 85, preferred_foot == 'Left') %>%
  summarise(best_left_footed_shooters = n())

best_shooters
```

#### Best bender

```{r}
best_bender <- players %>%
  select(short_name, skill_curve, preferred_foot) %>%
  filter(preferred_foot == 'Left') %>%
  arrange(desc(skill_curve)) %>%
  head(n = 1)

best_bender
```

#### Fastest player

```{r}
fastest_player <- players %>%
  filter(preferred_foot == 'Left') %>%
  slice_max(pace, n = 1) %>%
  select(long_name, club_name, pace)

fastest_player
```

#### Distribution skill attributes

```{r}
players %>%
  filter(preferred_foot == "Left") %>%
  summarise(
    dribbling_avg = mean(dribbling, na.rm = TRUE),
    shooting_avg = mean(shooting, na.rm = TRUE),
    pace_avg = mean(pace, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```
